title: Should investors continue to use AI providers as a Source of Funds for AI adopters?

Happy Wednesday out there, team 42. It's your skipper here, Darius Dale, to present our macro minute for Wednesday, June 24th, 2026. As always, we start with the executive summary prompt of today's lead-off morning note. So, let's dive right in. Today's numerical question is, should investors continue to use AI providers as a source of funds for AI adopters? The short answer is yes. Investors are starting to lean into our source of funds theme amid evidence of AI adopters pulling back on unproductive token burning amid too high cost for compute. The too high cost for compute will force a supply response among AI providers that ultimately lowers the prices of the goods and services that they sell at the expense of their historically high gross margins. In terms of the analytical nuance, Micron earnings tonight represent the most crucial microeconomic event of the year-to-date. This event follows three of June's five major market-moving events: the SpaceX IPO, the US-Iran memorandum of understanding, and Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's first FOMC event. The report follows a semiconductor sell-off being dubbed the chip wreck across global Wall Street. Recall that the SOX declined 8% yesterday. Uh the index is still up 89% from its March 30 lows. Elsewhere, chip maker Cerebras Systems shares are down 14% today on disappointing annual sales guidance. 2026 revenue is guided at 855 million to 855 to 865 million versus a Bloomberg consensus estimate of only 825 million. As an inspiring Nvidia rival, Cerebras faces lofty investor expectations rooted in the global AI data center build-out. Nvidia and a few chip makers routinely top estimates. So, even strong results like this no longer guarantee share price gains, something investors must be apprised of heading into Micron's results this evening. In other semiconductor news, SK Hynix aims to raise $29 billion via a landmark US listing that would rank among history's five biggest share sales, rivaling Saudi Aramco's 2019 IPO, which was a record at the time. In our view, AI providers are scrambling to acquire as quickly as possible because perhaps they sense the tide is turning from an investor demand perspective. The reopening of the straight of her moves and the resulting collapse in global energy prices represent a signal for some investors to book gains in the investing world's most crowded and most overbought trade. We've seen this source of funds movie before in global stock markets. Recall that Chinese stocks converge with US stocks as access to a unified global market diffuse throughout the Chinese economy. We've seen the source of funds movie before in global bond markets as well. Recall that European rates converged as the adoption of a unified monetary policy diffuse throughout Europe. As always we'll wrap up with the question from our community. This one's titled source of funds. Says can today's Russell outperformance versus MAGS and SPX be taken as an early hint of the source of funds trade skipper touched on in today's macro minutes or more of some more idiosyncratic effect involved. So I think it's all the same. I mean the reality is I mean the source of funds is not the only thing that's going to drive the relative performance of small caps relative to MAG 7 or S&P 500, but it's certainly a you know part of the is part of the story in our opinion. Again, the AI companies have to sell AI to the other companies in order to generate revenues and profits. I don't know exactly when the market's going to start you know pivoting its full sole focus away from the AI providers to when the AI to the AI adopters, but in our opinion we think that's going to be a you know multi-quarter multi-year process that has legs. You know it's not going to be all at once in our opinion, but it's going to be something that should have legs with respect to you know the longer term view on this. I mean there's you know they're spending 800 plus billion dollars of CAPEX on data centers and AI AI CAPEX in year 2026 with a projected growth rate to 1.2 trillion dollars of AI CAPEX next year. I think that's about 2 and 1/2% of nominal GDP this year inclusive of the growth estimates for Bloomberg consensus you're talking about another 3.6 3 to 4% of nominal GDP growth or nominal GDP next year, you know, they're going to have to find buyers for all that CapEx in order to generate a a rate of return. And and in order for them to generate a rate of return and find buyers, the folks in the, you know, the rest of the >> [laughter] >> the stock market, the companies in the rest of the stock market are going to have to generate rates of return on the tokens that they're using. And so, in our opinion, we think there's a looming productivity boom ahead of us, not just in the US economy, we're already seeing it. We're also seeing it in the global economy as well that ultimately will force the convergence of of margins, profitability, and ultimately valuations between some of these companies that have been left behind, not just in the most recent move in markets, but really for the last, you know, for 10 15 plus years in global capital markets. So, in our view, we think you're going to continue to see, you know, the south performance. It's not going to be, you know, persistent every single day, week, month, you know, there's going to be an outperformance of the AI adopters relative to the AI providers, but we certainly continue to view that, you know, on a multi-quarter, multi-year time horizon perspective. They were going to continue to see that that that trade play out. Again, this is very similar to the source of bunch trade that happened in in early 2000s with China relative to US, very similar to the source of bunch trade that we saw in the Europe across the peripheral bond markets in Europe converging to the German bond market by the late 1990s. And so, you know, check out our most recent macro scouting report, monthly macro scouting report presentation for some insights on that. So, we'll wrap it up there. Terry still here presenting our macro minute for June 24th, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We'll catch you back here tomorrow. Cheers.
